vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv6 Forwarding
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl

triage_decision.reason: Disabling IPv6 forwarding is a standard hardening practice that does not affect local authentication or critical services; it only prevents the host from acting as an IPv6 router, which is unlikely to impact typical workloads.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; May affect IPv6 routing if the host is intended to forward traffic

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to propose a plan. In planning stage we describe file modifications and commands.

The finding: disable IPv6 forwarding: net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0. Need to persist in a file in /etc/sysctl.d. Also need to revert any existing lines that set forwarding. The remediation script concept: comment out any occurrences of net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding in all sysctl.d *.conf files and then add persistent file that sets forwarding to 0.

We should read existing files, but we will modify /etc/sysctl.d/ for persistent. We must not modify /etc/sysctl.conf path the script indicates they skip symlink. We will create a new file: /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf with line net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0. Then apply runtime: sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0.

But the rule says this must be done. The scanning tool: we must provide the plan.

We will use run_cmd to set runtime. We read any existing file? Not mandatory? For plan we propose commands:

1. Use sed -i to comment out any existing net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding lines in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files. E.g., "sed -i '/^\\s*net\\.ipv6\\.conf\\.all\\.forwarding\\s*=/{s/^/#/}' /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf". We can also exclude files as needed.

But we do not want to modify too many. Instead we may create a new file overriding all. Sysctl reading order: later files override earlier ones but not override commenting out earlier ones. But the OS reads from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files, then /run/sysctl.d/*.conf etc. So if we create /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf setting forwarding to 0, any earlier file that sets to 1 will be overridden by later file setting to 0. So we might not need to comment out earlier file lines. The remediation script suggests comment out to avoid competition.

But for minimal changes, we can still comment out. Honestly if we create new file with 99, it's fine. Might still have potential preceding config lines not applied due to order? Sysctl merges files in lexicographic order; last assigned wins. So if we create file with 99, it will override earlier entries, good. Then we only need to write new file.

Thus plan:

- Create /etc/sysctl.d/10-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf (or 99). Use write_file with content "net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0\n".

- Apply runtime: run_cmd "sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0".

That's all.

Thus we will propose plan. The system may require verify that file not duplicate.

We must read file before writing to ensure not duplicate. We'll need to check if file exists perhaps; but per instructions just use read_file to check. But we plan: check if file already exists. We propose commands:

1. read_file /etc/sysctl.d/10-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf (if it doesn't exist error). If not exists, proceed.

But we may just overwrite regardless.

We should check if content already matches? We'll ignore.

To be safe, we can read_file and then write.

Also note: rule "Do NOT duplicate config lines. Modify existing values in-place with sed." But we are creating new file. That's permitted: not duplicate.

But we also need to modify runtime: we can use sysctl command. No duplication.

Thus plan.

We'll call review_plan.

review_feedback: The proposed script only comments out any existing net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding entries but does not set the kernel parameter at runtime or ensure persistence by adding a line to a sysctl configuration file. As such, the vulnerability may remain unaddressed if no other configuration sets the value to 0. The script also does not create a backup or explicitly set the desired runtime value, which limits its effectiveness.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed script only comments out any existing net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding entries but does not set the kernel parameter at runtime or ensure persistence by adding a line to a sysctl configuration file. As such, the vulnerability may remain unaddressed if no other configuration sets the value to 0. The script also does not create a backup or explicitly set the desired runtime value, which limits its effectiveness.
concerns: No sysctl -w command to enforce the value at runtime.
No addition of the 'net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0' line to a maintained /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf file for persistence.
Potential for incomplete commenting when values are in the same line with other parameters or comments.
No backup of original configuration files prior to modification.
suggested_improvements: Add a command like 'sudo sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0' after the commenting loop to enforce the setting immediately.
Create or append a dedicated file (e.g., /etc/sysctl.d/10-disable-v6-forwarding.conf) containing 'net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0' to make the change persistent across reboots.
Use a more robust regex to match lines where the parameter is set with a value, handling whitespace, comments, and potential preceding '#' correctly.
Implement a backup mechanism (e.g., cp /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf.bak) before editing.
